body:
Kundalini and Money: Why Financial Chaos Often Accompanies Awakening

You're probably reading this because you're in it. The thick of it. The spiritual awakening, the kundalini thing, whatever you want to call it. And somewhere in the swirling vortex of weird physical sensations, emotional breakdowns, and mind-bending shifts in perception, your money situation has gone to hell. Right? You're not alone. I've been there. I've seen it. It's not a coincidence, and it's not some cosmic punishment. It's part of the deal, often, for a lot of us. And it sucks. It feels isolating, terrifying, and frankly, like you're losing your mind on multiple fronts.

I remember being flat broke, living off credit cards, while simultaneously feeling like my nervous system was re-wiring itself with a sledgehammer. The world didn't make sense, my old job felt like a prison, and money, which used to be a stable if sometimes stressful part of life, became this terrifying, unstable monster. You're trying to figure out if you're going crazy, or if you're actually having a deep spiritual experience, and then you check your bank account and realize you can't even afford therapy to figure it out. It's a brutal irony, isn't it?

This isn't about blaming the awakening for your financial woes, not entirely. But it's about understanding the deep, often uncomfortable connection between these two seemingly unrelated aspects of your life. Because when you start to wake up, everything gets shaken up. And money, being such a fundamental part of how we operate in this world, is one of the first things to feel the tremor.

The Great Unraveling: Why Your Old Life Doesn't Fit

Look, when kundalini starts to move, or whatever energetic process you're going through, it's not just some nice, gentle spiritual glow-up. It's often a violent, chaotic demolition of everything you thought you were, everything you thought you needed, and everything you built your life around. And that includes your career, your values, and your relationship with money.

Suddenly, the job you tolerated for years feels like an existential drain. The ambition that drove you seems meaningless. The things you used to chase - bigger house, fancier car, more stuff - they just don't hold the same appeal. Or worse, they feel repulsive. You're seeing the matrix, you know? And the matrix is often built on a lot of things that don't align with this new, raw, authentic self that's trying to emerge. So you can't fake it anymore. You can't just show up and pretend you care about quarterly reports when you're having visions of the universe expanding or feeling the interconnectedness of all life. It's a clash, and your soul is going to win that battle, even if it means burning down your bank account in the process.

I remember trying to work during my worst periods. I'd sit at my desk, staring at the screen, and it was like trying to speak a foreign language I'd never learned. My brain just wasn't wired for that kind of linear, goal-oriented thinking anymore. It was too busy processing multi-dimensional data, you know? My focus was shot, my energy was erratic, and my patience for corporate bullshit was non-existent. It's not that I didn't want to make money; it's that the mechanism for making it in my old life had completely broken down. Maybe you're feeling something similar. You're not lazy; you're undergoing a deep internal transformation that simply isn't compatible with your old way of being in the world. Yet.

The Sensitivity Overload and Practical Impairment

One of the most immediate and debilitating effects of a strong kundalini activation is the sheer sensory and emotional overload. Your nervous system is basically on fire. You're more sensitive to everything - sounds, lights, people's energies, your own emotions. This isn't some woo-woo concept; it's a very real physiological experience. And it makes functioning in a normal, money-making capacity incredibly difficult.

Think about it: most jobs require a certain level of social interaction, focus, and ability to handle stress. When your system is already overwhelmed, just walking into a grocery store can feel like a heroic act. Sitting in an open-plan office? Forget about it. Dealing with demanding clients or a micromanaging boss? It's like pouring gasoline on an already raging fire. Your capacity for stress goes way down. Your ability to concentrate tanks. Your emotional regulation can be all over the place. This isn't you being weak; it's your system screaming for you to slow down, to protect yourself, to integrate what's happening. And that often means you can't perform at the same level you used to, or you can't perform at all. And guess what happens when you can't work? Money dries up. It's a harsh reality, but it's often the case.

I've known people who couldn't leave their beds for weeks, sometimes months, because the energy was so intense. How do you make a living when you're literally fighting for your sanity and basic physical comfort? You don't. Or you struggle immensely. This isn't a failure on your part. It's a symptom of the process. You're not broken; you're being rebuilt, and that's a messy, all-consuming job.

Re-evaluating Values: Money as a Mirror

Another big piece of this puzzle is the re-evaluation of your values. Before awakening, many of us are operating from a place where money equals security, status, or even self-worth. We're chasing external validation through material possessions or career achievements. But when the inner world explodes, those external markers often lose their luster. They feel hollow. They feel like distractions from the real work that's happening inside you.

You might start to question the entire capitalist system, the endless pursuit of more, the way society defines success. And when you question those things, it's hard to participate in them with the same drive and conviction. This isn't to say money is bad, or that you shouldn't have it. It's that your relationship with it changes. What you thought you wanted, you might not want anymore. What you thought was important, might now seem utterly trivial. This can lead to a period of deep confusion and disorientation around your financial goals and how you even want to earn a living.

It's like you're standing at a crossroads, and one path leads to your old life, your old job, your old financial stability - but it feels like a lie. The other path is completely unknown, scary, and might not have a clear income stream attached to it, but it feels authentic. Which one do you choose? Often, the awakening pushes you onto that unknown path, whether you're ready or not. And that means a period of financial instability is almost inevitable as you figure out how to align your new values with a way to sustain yourself in the world. It's terrifying, I know. But it's also an opportunity to build something truly aligned with who you're becoming.

The Purging of Old Energy and Trauma

Here's the thing: kundalini, or any deep spiritual awakening, isn't just about pretty visions and enlightenment. It's a deep purification process. It digs up everything that's not truly you - old traumas, repressed emotions, limiting beliefs, inherited patterns. And a huge chunk of those patterns are often tied to money. Money trauma is real. Scarcity mindset, fear of poverty, feelings of unworthiness, beliefs about money being "dirty" or "evil" - these are all stored in your system. And the awakening process will drag them kicking and screaming into your conscious awareness.

When these old wounds surface, they don't just sit there politely. They create chaos. They manifest in your external reality. So if you have deep-seated fears about not having enough, guess what? The universe might just give you a period of "not enough" to force you to confront those fears head-on. If you have unconscious beliefs that you don't deserve abundance, you might find yourself sabotaging opportunities or pushing money away. This isn't punishment; it's a mirror. It's the universe showing you what needs to be healed within you.

It's incredibly painful to go through this. You might feel like you're being tested, or that you're failing. But you're not. You're processing generations of crap, often. You're clearing out the energetic blockages that have prevented you from truly thriving, not just financially, but in all areas of your life. It's like a deep clean. And deep cleans are always messy before they're sparkling. The books The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) and The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) really get into the intensity of this purging process, how it can feel like you're losing your mind, and how it often impacts every part of your life, including your ability to function normally.

Integration: Building a New Foundation

So what do you do about it? How do you survive this financial chaos when you're already feeling like you're barely hanging on? There's no magic bullet, I won't lie to you. It's hard. But there are ways to deal with it, and ways to understand it so you don't feel so utterly alone and crazy.

First, acknowledge what's happening. Don't resist the unraveling. Lean into it, as scary as that sounds. The more you fight the changes, the more painful they'll be. This doesn't mean you just give up on money entirely, but it means accepting that your old strategies might not work right now. You might need to radically simplify your life. Cut expenses wherever you can. Ask for help from friends or family if you have a support system. Swallow your pride. This isn't a time for ego; it's a time for survival and deep self-care.

Second, prioritize your well-being. Your nervous system needs to be regulated. That means rest, gentle movement, nourishing food, time in nature, and anything that brings you a sense of peace. If you're constantly in fight-or-flight over money, you're just adding more stress to an already overloaded system. Try to find small ways to create a sense of safety, even if it's just making sure you have one good meal a day or a warm place to sleep. Sometimes, that's all you can manage. And that's okay. Bonnie Greenwell's book, When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening (paid link), and her other work, The Kundalini Guide (paid link), are excellent resources for understanding the practicalities of self-care during these intense periods. She really gets it.

Third, get creative with income. Your old job might not work, but maybe there are other ways to bring in some money that are less demanding. Can you do freelance work from home? Can you sell things you no longer need? Can you offer a skill you have on a small scale? Even tiny amounts can make a difference and help you feel less powerless. This isn't about becoming a millionaire overnight; it's about finding ways to bridge the gap while you're in this intense transitional phase. And it might mean accepting that your income will be less than before, for a while. That's a bitter pill to swallow, I know. But sometimes, less is truly more when your system can't handle the demands of a high-pressure job.

Fourth, work on your money beliefs. This is where the purging comes in. As the old fears and traumas surface, acknowledge them. Journal about them. Talk to someone you trust. See if you can gently reframe them. Instead of "I'm going to be broke forever," try "This is a temporary phase, and I'm learning to trust that my needs will be met." It's not about toxic positivity; it's about shifting your internal world even slightly, because your external reality will eventually reflect that. This is a long game, not a sprint.

Fifth, cultivate trust. This is probably the hardest part. Trust that you're being guided, even when it feels like you're being dragged through hell. Trust that there's a bigger plan. Trust that you're strong enough to get through this. You wouldn't be experiencing this if you weren't. You're being asked to surrender control, and that includes financial control. It's terrifying, but it's also where true freedom lies. It's a deep, deep lesson in faith, isn't it?

So yeah, if your finances are a mess during your awakening, you're not failing. You're not doing it wrong. You're probably doing it exactly right, in the most uncomfortable, terrifying way possible. It's part of the process of dismantling the old and making space for the new. Hold on. Breathe. Get support. You'll get through this. I promise you, you will. It won't be easy, but you'll emerge stronger, clearer, and with a completely different relationship to money and to life itself. Does that land? I hope so. Because I know how alone you feel right now. You're not.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
